agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Pi supports a native shell escape: any interactive-REPL input beginning with `!` is run as a local shell command WITHOUT an LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js line 2079 `if (text.startsWith("!"))` dispatches to handleBashCommand, which calls session.executeBash and recordBashResult; a leading `!!` additionally excludes the output from LLM context, per the startup banner hint 'rawKeyHint("!!", "to run bash (no context)")'). Pi records the escape as a `{"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}}` line in the rollout transcript — a user-side wrapper, NOT an assistant turn and NOT a formal toolCall/toolResult pair.

The scenario's literal spec wording (scenario-meanings.md Conceptual flow) is ready→working→ready: state transitions to working while the shell command runs, then back to ready. irrlicht does NOT reproduce that literal arc, because the pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) explicitly matches the `bashExecution` role and SKIPS it (`ev.Skip = true`) — confirmed by parser_test.go which asserts bashExecution is skipped and that a trailing bashExecution leaves LastEventType==turn_done unchanged. So no transcript_activity is recorded for the escape and no state_transition to working fires; the session stays ready across the shell escape.

irrlicht_observes is therefore `partial` (not `yes`): the daemon does NOT synthesize the working→ready arc the literal spec describes. What it DOES guarantee — and what this cell asserts as the intent reading — is the safety invariant that matters for a session-monitor: the shell escape does NOT leave the session stuck in working (no spurious working that never resolves) and does NOT emit a spurious waiting; the session remains ready and a subsequent real prompt still drives a clean ready→working→ready arc, proving the session is alive and uncorrupted. The alternative (literal) reading — synthesize a transition arc for the bash escape — would require a parser change to stop skipping bashExecution, which is out of scope for a recording.

Confidence 0.75: the supported behavior and the parser-skip are both directly verified in source + live binary, but the observable is a NEGATIVE invariant over the escape window (no working, no waiting, no stuck state) plus a positive real-prompt arc before it — strong but indirect proof that the escape was harmless.

WHY a warm-up real turn FIRST (before the escape): (1) pi creates its rollout-*.jsonl only after the first user message, so the driver's resolve_transcript needs a real turn to bind the UUID; (2) it supplies the one legitimate ready→working→ready arc that proves a NORMAL prompt still drives state, in contrast to the escape's silence.
caveats: irrlicht_observes is `partial`: the daemon does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command — the pi parser deliberately skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131). The cell adopts the INTENT reading: the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.

Shell-escape syntax is a leading `!` (verified pi 0.70.0: interactive-mode.js handleBashCommand). `!echo hi` runs locally with no LLM round-trip and writes a single bashExecution rollout line. `!!` is the no-context variant; either is skipped identically by the pi parser.

The observable for the escape itself is purely NEGATIVE (no second working, no waiting, no stuck state) — 'a shell escape was issued' is invisible to events.jsonl because the bashExecution wrapper is skipped. The warm-up real turn provides the positive ready→working→ready arc to contrast against the escape's silence.

The live REPL may briefly emit a proc-<PID> pre-session born ready before the UUID-keyed rollout transcript appears; the spec anchors session_birth to the pre-session-tolerant 'ready within 2s' match and pins the warm-up arc + post-escape dwell to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchored to recording start with no pin binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working).

sources.note: Lines 128-131: case "bashExecution" sets ev.Skip = true and returns — the user-side shell escape (! command) is deliberately skipped, so no state machinery fires and the session stays ready.

Lines 106-111 assert a bashExecution message is skipped; lines 482-505 assert that a bashExecution after an assistant stopReason=stop leaves LastEventType==turn_done unchanged (no working synthesized).

Line 2079 `if (text.startsWith("!"))` routes the input to handleBashCommand (line 4427) → session.executeBash + recordBashResult, with no LLM round-trip; `!!` (line 2080) excludes output from context. Startup banner advertises `!!` 'to run bash (no context)'. messages.js records role:"bashExecution" with command + output fields.

Interactive tmux REPL driver supporting send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep; a `send` step types `!echo hi` + Enter inside the live REPL to trigger the shell escape (no wait_turn after, since the escape produces no assistant stopReason=stop turn).

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY or configured provider; verified against pi 0.70.0)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
pi is launched in interactive REPL mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — headless `pi --print` is single-shot and has no interactive ! shell mode

INTENT READING (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): the pi parser deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131), so irrlicht does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command. This cell asserts the safety invariant instead — the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.
setup: Driver boots a fresh pi REPL in a per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; pi assigns its own session UUID per rollout

The shell escape is issued via a `send` step typing the literal text `!echo hi` + Enter (the pi driver's send handler uses tmux send-keys -l, so the leading ! is sent verbatim and pi runs it as a local shell command); NO wait_turn follows the escape, since `!`-prefixed input produces a bashExecution rollout line, not an assistant stopReason=stop turn

Avoid !! only if you want context inclusion to differ — either ! or !! is skipped identically by the pi parser; this recipe uses single ! (output retained in context, no LLM round-trip at escape time)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (pi's first message drives working, stopReason=stop drives ready); pi may flush the single warm-up turn as one OR two working→ready bursts

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — the !echo shell escape drives none because the pi parser skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the escape window (≥6s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the rollout session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for the !echo escape; the session is NOT left stuck in working

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user message line, 1 assistant stopReason=stop line (the warm-up turn), and 1 bashExecution line (the !echo escape — which does NOT appear as an assistant turn and is skipped by the parser)
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session; final session state is ready
recipe.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode/codex: pi runs `!`-prefixed REPL input as a LOCAL shell command with no LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js text.startsWith("!") → handleBashCommand → executeBash + recordBashResult; the `!!` variant additionally excludes output from context). Pi records the escape as ONE {"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}} rollout line. The pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution role (ev.Skip = true), so — UNLIKE codex, which synthesizes a working→ready arc for its !-escape, and LIKE claudecode, which skips its <bash-input> wrappers — the daemon sees no transcript_activity and the session stays ready across the escape. The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a second working transition plus the proof (warm-up arc) that a NORMAL prompt still drives state. RECIPE SHAPE: the warm-up turn comes FIRST (the escape SECOND) — opposite to the claudecode recipe — because pi creates ~/.pi/agent/sessions/.../<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl only after the first user message, so without a leading real turn the driver's resolve_transcript never binds a UUID. The escape uses `send` (not the claudecode `keys` step): the pi interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys, and step_send types the literal text (-l) then Enter, so `!echo hi` is delivered verbatim. NO wait_turn after the escape: it produces no assistant stopReason=stop, so wait_turn would time out; the trailing 6s sleep instead holds the recording open past the classifier debounce so the post-escape ready dwell is observed cleanly and the daemon-kill doesn't race the final-state emission. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears); session_birth legitimately matches that pre-session, while the warm-up arc and post-escape dwell pin to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchors to recording start with NO pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working). Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ, and pi may flush the warm-up turn as one or two working→ready bursts; the load-bearing assertion is the silent ready dwell across the escape window. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (case "bashExecution" → ev.Skip) and whether a pi update changed how `!` escapes are recorded in the rollout.
